content.text:
this should work right, or did the syntax change: 

❯ go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
    --protocol v2 \
    --measure-memory \
    -v \
    --target-username git \
    --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
    --tags \
    https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes.git \
    https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-8
error: list target refs: pkt-line 1: cannot read hash, pkt-line too short (# service=git-receive-pack)
exit status 1
I’m checking the current CLI and sync path in this repo first, then I’ll trace where that `git-receive-pack` pkt-line error is coming from to tell you whether this is syntax or protocol behavior.

The flags are still present, so this is not a simple flag rename. I’m checking whether `sync` still accepts positional URLs and whether the failure is actually the target speaking the wrong Git service on the wire.

The CLI still accepts your exact flag names and also still accepts positional `<source-url> <target-url>`. The error points at target ref discovery, so I’m narrowing this to the HTTP/Git service mismatch rather than argument parsing.

input.cmd: rg -n "list target refs|cannot read hash|git-receive-pack|service=git-receive-pack|pkt-line too short" internal cmd -g'*.go'

content.text: The target side is discovered with a `receive-pack` `info/refs` call, exactly where your error says it breaks. I’m checking the HTTP transport code next to see what response shape it expects and why `# service=git-receive-pack` is being treated as malformed.

I’ve confirmed one important detail from this codebase: `--protocol v2` only affects the source side. The target is always queried via v1 `receive-pack` discovery, so your failure is unrelated to target protocol selection and more likely means the target endpoint returned an unexpected smart-HTTP advertisement.

The repo’s own docs still describe this Entire-local path as supported, including `--protocol v2` against a local Entire target. That makes the current failure look even more like a server-side advertisement quirk or a client-side decoder edge case, not a CLI syntax drift.

content.text: I have enough to answer the syntax question. I’m doing one last check against the target endpoint shape so I can tell you whether the command itself is valid but the server response is malformed.

Your command syntax is still valid.

`sync` still accepts:
- `--protocol`
- `--measure-memory`
- `-v`
- `--target-username`
- `--target-insecure-skip-tls-verify`
- `--tags`
- positional `<source-url> <target-url>`

That’s in [cmd/git-sync/main.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/cmd/git-sync/main.go:55) and the positional URL fallback is in [cmd/git-sync/main.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/cmd/git-sync/main.go:84).

The failure is lower-level than CLI parsing. `sync` is dying while listing target refs via target `receive-pack` discovery in [internal/syncer/syncer.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/syncer.go:369). So this is the target endpoint or its response shape, not a changed flag.

Two useful details:
- `--protocol v2` only applies to the source side here; target discovery is still v1 `receive-pack` smart HTTP. That matches the README and code in [internal/gitproto/refs.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/gitproto/refs.go:21).
- The specific error suggests the target returned an unexpected `info/refs?service=git-receive-pack` advertisement, or auth/endpoint routing caused a non-standard response to be parsed as Git pkt-lines.

Most likely things to check:
- target URL is the actual Git smart-HTTP repo endpoint for push, not a UI/API route
- target auth is present; for Entire that usually means `--target-token` or `--target-bearer-token`, unless your local credential lookup is configured
- the server’s `GET <target>/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack` response is a valid smart-HTTP advertisement

So: syntax did not change. The command is fine; the target `receive-pack` discovery is what looks wrong.
this is the exact same command that worked before, same source, same target (but new repo)

Then the likely change is not CLI syntax and not the source side. It’s one of these:

- the new target repo is being served differently when empty
- the target auth/resolution path changed for this repo
- the target’s `receive-pack` `info/refs` response regressed

The error is very specific: `git-sync` got as far as target ref discovery and then failed decoding the `git-receive-pack` advertisement. In this codebase, that happens before any push logic. A brand-new repo is the strongest clue: empty-target handling on the server side is the thing that differs from your previously working repo.

What I’d check first:

- Run `git ls-remote https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-8`
  - if that fails, the repo endpoint itself is wrong or not fully initialized
- Run a raw check against:
  - `GET https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-8/info/refs?service=git-receive-pack`
  - it should return a valid smart-HTTP advertisement, not an HTML page, JSON error, or truncated pkt-line stream
- Compare the new repo with an older repo that worked:
  - same path shape
  - same auth requirements
  - same backend initialization for empty repos

One more important point: your command only sets `--target-username git`, not a token. That can still work if credentials are being discovered implicitly, but if the new repo is behind a slightly different auth path, the server may be returning something non-Git and the pkt-line decoder is choking on it.

So I would not change the command syntax. I would debug the new repo’s `receive-pack` advertisement and auth path. If you want, I can patch `git-sync` to log the first bytes of the target `info/refs` response on decode failure so this becomes obvious immediately.
we did do a pretty big rewrite of the code base since I last run this, so maybe any idea where this could have been broken?

I’m looking for places where the rewrite changed target discovery or empty-target behavior, because the error occurs before any actual sync work. The strongest suspects are the new target `receive-pack` probe path and how empty advertisements are decoded.

input.cmd: rg -n "empty target|bootstrap opportunity|list target refs|AdvertisedRefsV1|receive-pack" internal/syncer internal/gitproto -g'*.go'
git log --oneline -- cmd/git-sync/main.go internal/syncer/syncer.go internal/gitproto/refs.go internal/gitproto/smarthttp.go | head -n 30

content.text: The rewrite likely moved the break into session setup. I’m checking the refactor commits around `newSession` and target discovery, because that’s where the current failure path was introduced.

Yes. The most plausible break is in the rewritten target setup path, not the CLI.

Best candidate:
- [internal/syncer/syncer.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/syncer.go:365)
  - `newSession()` now always does target ref discovery up front with `gitproto.AdvertisedRefsV1(..., transport.ReceivePackService)`.
  - Your error happens exactly there: `list target refs`.

Why this is suspicious:
- this runs before the bootstrap/empty-target decision in [internal/syncer/syncer.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/syncer.go:417)
- so if the new repo’s empty `receive-pack` advertisement is slightly unusual, the sync dies before it can decide “empty target, use bootstrap relay”

Second candidate:
- [internal/gitproto/refs.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/gitproto/refs.go:68)
  - `AdvertisedRefsV1()` calls `RequestInfoRefs()` and then `decodeV1AdvRefs()`
- [internal/gitproto/refs.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/gitproto/refs.go:157)
  - `decodeV1AdvRefs()` just hands the raw body to `packp.AdvRefs.Decode`

That decoder path is brittle if Entire now returns any nonstandard-but-close `receive-pack` response for a fresh repo:
- banner-only
- missing flush after `# service=git-receive-pack`
- empty repo advertisement shaped differently from the in-memory test server
- auth/error body that still starts with Git-ish text

Third candidate:
- [internal/gitproto/smarthttp.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/gitproto/smarthttp.go:78)
  - `RequestInfoRefs()` no longer validates response content type or logs bad bodies
  - so a misrouted HTML/JSON/auth response becomes a pkt-line decode error, which is exactly the kind of opaque failure you saw

So if I had to bet, I’d put it on:
1. `newSession()` always probing target `receive-pack`
2. `decodeV1AdvRefs()` not tolerating an empty/new Entire repo advertisement shape

What I would change first:
- on target `receive-pack` decode failure, log the first 200 raw bytes and content type
- if the response is effectively “service banner + no refs”, treat it as empty target instead of fatal
- add an integration test for an empty target whose `receive-pack` `info/refs` is banner-only or otherwise sparse

If you want, I can make that diagnostic patch now and likely also harden empty-target handling in the same spot.
yes, do this

I’m patching the target discovery path now. First I’m checking how the underlying `packp` decoder treats sparse advertisements so I can make the fallback precise instead of papering over unrelated decode failures.

input.cmd: rg -n "cannot read hash|pkt-line too short|ErrEmptyAdvRefs|service=" $(go env GOPATH)/pkg/mod -g'*.go'

content.text: I found a concrete weakness: `decodeV1AdvRefs()` currently feeds the raw `info/refs` body straight into `packp.AdvRefs.Decode`, while go-git’s own HTTP transport first strips the smart-HTTP `# service=...` header and tolerates empty advertisements after that. I’m aligning our decoder with that behavior and adding a focused regression test.

I’m editing the decoder now. The change is small but targeted: normalize smart-HTTP v1 advertisements the way go-git’s transport does, then preserve a short raw-body preview in failure messages so the next mismatch is immediately visible.
The decoder patch is in and formatting is clean. I’m running the gitproto and syncer test suites now to make sure the new empty-advertisement handling didn’t change normal behavior.

content.text: The one failing test is useful: after a valid smart header plus flush, `packp.AdvRefs.Decode` returns `empty input` instead of `ErrEmptyAdvRefs` on this library version. I’m tightening that case so a banner-only empty target is treated as empty remote, which is the behavior we want.
`internal/gitproto` is green with the new regression tests. I’m waiting for `internal/syncer` to finish so we know the broader sync flow still behaves the same.

content.text:
I changed the v1 advertisement decoder in [internal/gitproto/refs.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/gitproto/refs.go:155) so it now:
- consumes the smart-HTTP `# service=...` header explicitly before decoding refs
- treats a header-only / banner-only advertisement as an empty remote instead of a hard decode failure
- includes a short raw body preview on malformed decode errors

I also added regression coverage in [internal/gitproto/refs_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/gitproto/refs_test.go:128) for:
- smart empty `receive-pack` advertisements
- malformed bodies that should surface a preview in the error

Verification: `go test ./internal/gitproto ./internal/syncer` passed.

This should address the most likely rewrite regression for a brand-new target repo. Re-run the same `go run ./cmd/git-sync sync ...` command; if it still fails, the new error should now show the first bytes of the target `info/refs` response, which will make the next issue obvious.
what kind of integration tests do we have and could one of catched this?

I’m scanning the current integration coverage and comparing it to this failure mode. I want to answer two things precisely: what is already covered, and whether any existing test should have caught a smart-HTTP empty-target regression like this.

The repo already has three layers of integration coverage: in-process smart-HTTP integration, real `git-http-backend` end-to-end tests, and optional Entire-local smoke tests. I’m checking whether the Exact bug path sits in a gap between those layers or whether we simply weren’t running the right test regularly.

content.text: I’ve got the answer. There is decent integration coverage, but this exact regression sat in a seam: the default in-process tests didn’t model the smart-HTTP `# service=...` envelope closely enough, and the Entire smoke is optional rather than part of the default suite.

There are three meaningful test layers right now.

`1.` Default suite: in-process integration in [internal/syncer/integration_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/integration_test.go:40).  
This is the biggest body of coverage. It exercises:
- initial sync to empty target
- bootstrap relay and batched bootstrap
- incremental relay
- branch mapping, tags, prune, force
- protocol v2 on the source
- push/fetch mid-stream failures
- dry-run, stats, credential-helper fallback

`2.` Real local smart-HTTP E2E via `git-http-backend` in [internal/syncer/git_http_backend_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/git_http_backend_test.go:23).  
This uses actual `git-http-backend` against local bare repos, so it is much closer to production smart HTTP than the in-process server.

`3.` Optional live smokes:
- local Linux source smoke in [internal/syncer/live_bootstrap_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/live_bootstrap_test.go:13)
- Entire-local smoke in [internal/syncer/entire_local_smoke_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/entire_local_smoke_test.go:32)

Could one of them have caught this?

Yes, but with an important caveat.

The one that should have caught it best is the Entire-local smoke in [internal/syncer/entire_local_smoke_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/entire_local_smoke_test.go:32), because your failure was specifically on a real Entire target and specifically on a fresh repo. If that test had been run after the rewrite, there is a good chance it would have failed.

The `git-http-backend` tests also might have caught it, because they use real smart HTTP. But they are optional, not part of the default `go test ./...` path, per [docs/testing.md](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/docs/testing.md:11). If they were not being run routinely, they would not protect you.

Why the default suite missed it:
- the in-process server in [internal/syncer/integration_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/integration_test.go:1876) uses `transport.AdvertiseReferences(...)` directly
- that did not expose the exact smart-HTTP framing assumption our decoder had wrong
- so the tests covered “empty target bootstrap works” logically, but not the exact wire-format edge on `receive-pack` discovery from a real server

So the short answer is:
- default tests: probably no
- `git-http-backend` E2E: maybe
- Entire-local smoke: yes, most likely

The new regression test I added in [internal/gitproto/refs_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/gitproto/refs_test.go:128) now catches this decoder class directly, which is the right permanent fix. If you want to raise the bar further, the next best addition would be a `git-http-backend` or custom integration test for an empty target `receive-pack` advertisement with smart-header-only framing.

the kubernetes test worked now, but now I did this one:

❯ go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
    --protocol v2 \
    --measure-memory \
    -v \
    --target-username git \
    --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
    https://github.com/torvalds/linux.git \
    https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-8


and it went to 74GB ram and then I had to kill it

I’m checking why this run stopped using the low-memory relay path. The likely causes are “all branches” semantics or a fallback from bootstrap/incremental relay into the materialized object-store path, so I’m tracing those conditions now.

input.cmd: rg -n "CanBootstrapRelay|CanIncrementalRelay|materialized|max-objects|no-thin|batch.*fallback|all source branches|default is all source branches" README.md docs internal -g'*.go' -g'*.md'

content.text:
That behavior makes sense with your exact sequence.

You first synced Kubernetes into `git-sync-smoke-8`, then reused the same target repo for Linux. At that point the target was no longer empty, so `sync` could not take the empty-target bootstrap relay path in [internal/syncer/syncer.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/syncer.go:418).

After that, the current `sync` flow does this for non-empty targets in [internal/syncer/syncer.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/syncer.go:429):
- allocate an in-memory repo
- fetch desired source objects into memory
- only then build plans / detect blocks / maybe fall back

Two things made it blow up:

- You omitted `--branch`, so `sync` defaults to all source branches.
- The target already contained Kubernetes refs, so this was not a cheap initial seed. It became a giant non-empty reconciliation against an unrelated repo.

So the high-probability explanation is:
- empty-target relay was unavailable because the repo already had Kubernetes in it
- `sync` fetched a huge Linux object graph into memory before it could conclude what to do
- that drove RAM to 74 GB

What to do instead:
- use a fresh empty target repo for Linux
- or restrict it explicitly, e.g. `--branch master`
- and for safety, set a lower `--materialized-max-objects` so it fails early instead of consuming the machine

For example:

```bash
go run ./cmd/git-sync sync \
  --protocol v2 \
  --measure-memory \
  --materialized-max-objects 100000 \
  --branch master \
  -v \
  --target-username git \
  --target-insecure-skip-tls-verify \
  https://github.com/torvalds/linux.git \
  https://localhost:8080/git/test/git-sync-smoke-linux
```

There is also a real product issue here: `sync` currently fetches before it knows the run is obviously a bad non-empty-target/unrelated-history case. That is something we should harden. I can patch it to fail much earlier when a non-empty target points at clearly unrelated refs, or at least add a stricter preflight for large non-empty runs.
yeah, let's add a fix to prevent this earlier

I’m looking for the narrowest fix that blocks this class of run before it allocates tens of gigabytes. The main choices are a stricter preflight in `sync`, or enforcing the object-count safety rail during fetch instead of only after materializing everything.

The current safety limit is too late: it runs after we’ve already fetched and stored the pack in memory. I’m checking whether we can enforce the object-count limit during unpack, which would stop pathological runs much earlier without guessing based on branch names.

There’s a workable path here: intercept object writes during pack unpack and stop once the configured object budget is exceeded. I’m checking the exact storage hooks go-git uses so the guard trips during fetch, not after the pack is fully resident.

I’m implementing the guard at the real choke point: the in-memory fetch store. That way `sync`/`plan` will abort during unpack once the object budget is exceeded, instead of finishing a huge fetch and only failing later.
could we also just check the oldest ref if it's matching and fail if it's not?

That heuristic is too brittle. Git remotes don’t cheaply expose “oldest shared ref” in a way that distinguishes “same repo, older target” from “different repo with a reused branch name”, so it would produce false positives on normal lagging targets and false negatives on unrelated repos.

I’m sticking with the safer fix: abort during in-memory fetch once the materialized object budget is exceeded. That stops the blow-up regardless of branch names or history shape.

I’ve wired the guard into `sync`’s materialized fetch path. I’m tightening one detail before tests: the same protection should apply to `plan`/dry-run too, because they use the same in-memory fetch path.

I’m adding focused tests around the new guard rather than relying only on an integration failure string. One test will check the wrapper directly, and one existing integration test will be updated to assert the earlier failure mode.
The guard and tests are in. I’m formatting and running the affected suites now to catch any interface mismatch or unexpected fetch behavior before you try it again.

content.text:
I added an earlier guard in the materialized fetch path, so `sync` and `plan` now abort during pack unpack once the in-memory object budget is exceeded, instead of fetching everything and blowing up first.

The main change is in [internal/syncer/syncer.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/syncer.go:437), where the source fetch now writes through a limiting wrapper from [internal/syncer/limited_storer.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/limited_storer.go:1). That wrapper counts objects as they are stored and returns a clear error like “materialized fetch exceeded object limit…”. I also added focused tests in [internal/syncer/limited_storer_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/limited_storer_test.go:1) and updated the existing integration expectation in [internal/syncer/integration_test.go](/Users/soph/Work/entire/devenv/git-sync/internal/syncer/integration_test.go:127).

Verification: `go test ./internal/syncer ./cmd/git-sync` passed.

This does not use the “oldest ref matches” heuristic. I skipped that because it would reject legitimate lagging targets and still miss some unrelated-history cases. The object-budget guard is more reliable. Re-run the Linux command and it should now fail early with a bounded error instead of consuming the machine.
